name: Abhijit Banerjee & Esther Duflo
identity: MIT economists who tested anti-poverty ideas with field experiments, and won the 2019 Nobel for it.

name: Abraham Maslow
identity: American psychologist; founder of humanistic psychology

name: Adam Grant
identity: American organisational psychologist at Wharton whose work on giving and taking reframed who really gets ahead.

name: Alasdair MacIntyre
identity: Scottish-American moral philosopher who argued that a human action — and a human life — is intelligible only as part of a story.

name: Albert Bandura
identity: Canadian-American psychologist whose idea of self-efficacy — belief in one's ability to do a specific thing — became one of psychology's most-tested concepts.

name: Amy Edmondson
identity: Harvard Business School professor who named and proved psychological safety — the team condition that lets people admit error and ask for help.

name: Antony Jay
identity: British writer and management observer who read the corporation as a small state, run by faction and alliance.

name: Atul Gawande
identity: Surgeon and writer who showed that a short, plain checklist can sharply cut deaths in the operating room. Experts fail not from ignorance but from forgetting.

name: BJ Fogg
identity: Stanford behaviour scientist who showed that a behaviour needs three things at once — and that the most reliable one to design for is how easy the act is.

name: Brian Boyd
identity: New Zealand literary scholar who argues that storytelling is not a cultural luxury but an evolved adaptation — a safe way to rehearse life.

name: Brigitte Madrian & Dennis Shea
identity: The economists who proved that the default option, not persuasion, decides what most people do.

name: C. Northcote Parkinson
identity: British naval historian who, in a 1955 satirical essay, named the patterns that make bureaucracies grow and meetings waste their time.

name: Carol Dweck
identity: Stanford psychologist whose mindset theory split how people read their own ability into two camps, fixed and growth.

name: Charles Duhigg
identity: American journalist and author; the writer who turned habit science into a working vocabulary for change.

name: Charlie Munger
identity: Warren Buffett's partner at Berkshire Hathaway, and the practitioner who turned thinking backward — and thinking across disciplines — into a working method.

name: Chip & Dan Heath
identity: Brothers and writing partners. They turned a generation of behavioural research into change advice that leaders actually remember.

name: Confucius
identity: A Chinese teacher and political advisor of the Spring and Autumn period. His recorded conversations, the Analects, have shaped East Asian thought, governance, and family life for two and a half thousand years.

name: Cristina Bicchieri
identity: Italian-American philosopher who showed that what we loosely call “a norm” is really three different things, each changed a different way.

name: Dan McAdams
identity: Personality psychologist; the leading figure in narrative identity theory.

name: Daniel Kahneman & Amos Tversky
identity: Israeli psychologists whose collaboration founded behavioural economics and reshaped how we understand judgement under risk.

name: Daniel Pink
identity: American author and former Gore speechwriter who turned the science of motivation into a vocabulary working managers could use.

name: Daryl Conner
identity: American change consultant who gave the field the “burning platform.” It names the felt conviction that staying still now costs more than moving.

name: Dean Karlan
identity: The development economist who showed that a thirty-cent text message can lift saving. The gap between intention and action is often just memory.

name: Don Norman
identity: American cognitive scientist and design theorist who showed that most of what we call user error is design error.

name: Donald Miller
identity: American writer and founder of the marketing company StoryBrand.

name: Donald Schön
identity: American philosopher of professional practice who showed that skilled work is a live conversation with the situation, not the application of theory.

name: Donella Meadows
identity: Environmental scientist and the clearest writer on systems thinking, the discipline of seeing the structure beneath behaviour.

name: Duncan Watts
identity: Network scientist who tests how things actually spread

name: Edgar Schein
identity: Organisational psychologist who named the two fears that decide whether people change, and showed why fear alone freezes them.

name: Edward Deci & Richard Ryan
identity: The University of Rochester pair who built self-determination theory, the modern account of where lasting motivation comes from.

name: Edwin Locke & Gary Latham
identity: The two psychologists behind goal-setting theory — the most replicated finding on what makes a goal move people.

name: Elliott Jaques
identity: Canadian psychoanalyst and management theorist who showed that the right number of organisational layers is set by how far ahead each level has to think.

name: Eric Johnson & Daniel Goldstein
identity: Decision scientists who showed, with organ-donor data, that the single most powerful lever over a choice is what you set as the default.

name: Everett Rogers
identity: The sociologist who explained how new ideas spread. Through people, in stages, not all at once.

name: Francis Fukuyama
identity: American political scientist. He wrote the fullest recent account of how durable, impersonal institutions get built, and how they decay.

name: Franz Kafka
identity: The Prague writer who gave the modern world its words for what bureaucratic life feels like from the inside.

name: Frederick Herzberg
identity: American psychologist who showed that the things which stop people being unhappy at work are different in kind from the things that make them care.

name: Gabriele Oettingen
identity: German-American psychologist who showed that picturing a goal can sap the drive to reach it. She then built a four-step fix.

name: Geoffrey Moore
identity: American marketing consultant whose 1991 book Crossing the Chasm named the most-cited failure mode in how new things spread.

name: George Loewenstein
identity: American economist and psychologist, one of the founders of behavioural economics.

name: George Orwell
identity: British essayist, novelist, and journalist

name: Hal Hershfield
identity: Behavioural scientist at UCLA Anderson; the leading researcher on how we relate to our future selves.

name: Han Fei
identity: The most systematic thinker of Chinese Legalism. He argued that good government rests not on the virtue of rulers but on clear law, reliable enforcement, and honest incentives. His collected writings, the Han Feizi, became a working manual of statecraft.

name: Hannah Arendt
identity: German-American political theorist who argued that who a person is becomes visible only through action — and through the stories others tell of it.

name: Henry Mintzberg
identity: Management theorist at McGill who watched what managers actually do. He found the textbook almost entirely wrong.

name: Howard Gardner
identity: Harvard developmental psychologist; author of the foundational academic study of how leaders use story.

name: James C. Scott
identity: The political scientist who explained why grand plans imposed from above keep failing the same way.

name: James Clear
identity: Author of Atomic Habits

name: James March (with Barbara Levitt)
identity: One of the founders of modern organisation theory; the scholar who explained how organisations remember and why success traps them.

name: James Pennebaker
identity: The social psychologist who showed that writing a hard experience into a coherent story measurably improves health.

name: James Prochaska & Carlo DiClemente
identity: Two psychologists who showed that people changing a behaviour do not all sit at the same point. Readiness moves through stages, and help has to match the stage.

name: James Q. Wilson
identity: American political scientist whose 1989 study of how government agencies behave is the most useful single account of large bureaucracies.

name: James Womack & Daniel Jones
identity: MIT researchers who named and codified lean, carrying Toyota's production system into Western practice.

name: Jane Jacobs
identity: Self-taught writer on cities and institutions who showed that the moral codes of government and of business are two different things.

name: Jay Forrester
identity: American engineer who founded system dynamics, the modelling of feedback, stocks, and flows in human systems.

name: Jeffrey Pfeffer
identity: Stanford organisational theorist who studies power inside organisations. He looks at how it is won, used, and resisted.

name: Jerome Bruner
identity: American psychologist who led the cognitive revolution and put storytelling back at the centre of how the mind makes sense of the world.

name: John Gall
identity: American paediatrician whose wry 1975 book on why systems fail gave the field one of its most durable laws.

name: John Kotter
identity: Harvard Business School professor and the most-cited modern writer on leading organisational change.

name: John Meyer & Brian Rowan
identity: Stanford sociologists who showed that organisations keep formal structures alive to look right to outsiders, not to do the work.

name: John Shook
identity: American industrial engineer who spent over a decade inside Toyota. He wrote the clearest account we have of how culture actually changes. It changes when people change what they do, not what they believe.

name: Joseph Campbell
identity: American mythologist who found one deep story shared across the world’s myths — the hero’s journey — and made it the best-known account of narrative shape in modern Western culture.

name: Julian Orr
identity: American anthropologist whose 1996 ethnography of Xerox copier technicians showed that real work always exceeds the documented procedure. The gap is held together by storytelling.

name: Karl Weick
identity: American organisational psychologist. He showed that people in organisations act first and make sense of it after, and that the sense they make takes the form of a story.

name: Kautilya
dates: compiled c. 4th century BCE – 3rd century CE
identity: Founding author of the Arthashastra, the ancient world’s most complete manual for running a state. It carries the first sustained treatment of how to keep officials honest.

name: Kurt Lewin
identity: Founder of modern social psychology and of the field of organisational change.

name: Kurt Vonnegut and the Computational Story Lab
identity: A novelist's sketch of the shapes stories take, confirmed half a century later by a lab that measured 1,327 of them.

name: Lawrence Lessig
identity: Harvard legal scholar who named the four forces that regulate any behaviour, and showed that design has quietly become the strongest of them.

name: Lee Kuan Yew
identity: Founding prime minister of Singapore; the architect of a state built, against the odds, on merit and an incorruptible civil service.

name: Leidy Klotz
identity: Engineer at the University of Virginia who showed, experimentally, that people reach for adding and overlook taking away.

name: Mark Granovetter
identity: American sociologist whose work showed how the shape of a social network governs what spreads through it.

name: Marshall Ganz
identity: Veteran community organiser turned Harvard scholar of leadership, organising, and the public story that moves people to act.

name: Max Weber
identity: German sociologist and a founder of the discipline. He gave the modern world its account of what bureaucracy is and why it spreads.

name: Michael Hammer & James Champy
identity: The two authors of business process reengineering. They launched the clean-sheet redesign movement that ruled corporate change for a decade. Then it became the most fully documented failure in management.

name: Michael Polanyi
identity: Hungarian-British chemist-turned-philosopher who named the knowledge a skilled person has but cannot put into words.

name: Michael White and David Epston
identity: The two clinicians who built narrative therapy — the practice of treating a person's problem as a story that can be separated from them and re-written.

name: Michel Crozier
identity: French sociologist of organisations; the sharpest twentieth-century account of why bureaucracies defeat themselves.

name: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
identity: Hungarian-American psychologist who named flow, the absorbed state where a hard task becomes its own reward. He co-founded positive psychology.

name: Mike Rother
identity: American engineer who found what made Toyota different. Not its tools, but two practised routines.

name: Niccolò Machiavelli
identity: Florentine diplomat and civil servant who founded the Western tradition of seeing politics as it is, not as it should be.

name: Paul Attewell
identity: American sociologist whose 1992 study showed that know-how, not awareness, paces the adoption of a new technology.

name: Paul DiMaggio & Walter Powell
identity: Two American sociologists. Their 1983 paper explained why competing firms in the same industry end up looking alike, and why their worst shared routines are the hardest to remove.

name: Paul Lazarsfeld & Elihu Katz
identity: Sociologists who found, by counting, that mass media rarely changes minds on its own. It reaches a few attentive people who then pass a digested version to everyone else through ordinary conversation.

name: Peter Gollwitzer
identity: German social psychologist whose “implementation intention” turns a wish into a reliable behaviour. You name when and where it will happen.

name: Peter Senge
identity: MIT systems scientist who carried system dynamics out of the lab and into the everyday language of management.

name: Pierre Bourdieu
identity: French sociologist who explained how social structures get carried inside people — and reproduced through everyday practice.

name: René Girard
identity: French literary critic and anthropologist who argued that we do not choose our desires — we catch them from each other.

name: Richard Beckhard & Reuben Harris
identity: Organisation-development pioneers who put the field’s most widely used change formula into print.

name: Richard Dawkins
identity: British evolutionary biologist who coined the word “meme.” His idea is that culture spreads and evolves the way genes do.

name: Richard Nelson & Sidney Winter
identity: The economists who founded evolutionary economics and named the routine as the basic unit of how a firm behaves.

name: Richard Thaler & Cass Sunstein
identity: An economist and a legal scholar whose 2008 book Nudge showed that the way a choice is arranged quietly shapes what people pick.

name: Robert Caro
identity: American journalist and biographer; the closest thing the study of power has to a modern chronicler.

name: Robert Cialdini
identity: American social psychologist whose work codified the science of influence and persuasion.

name: Robert Greene
identity: American writer whose books gather the old literature of power and strategy into a modern, usable form.

name: Robert Kegan & Lisa Lahey
identity: Harvard developmental psychologists who explained why capable, willing people still fail to change. They also built a method to free them.

name: Robert Putnam
identity: American political scientist who gave the modern world the idea of social capital.

name: Ronald Heifetz
identity: The Harvard teacher who named the kind of change that asks people to give something up. He also named why leading it is dangerous.

name: Rosabeth Moss Kanter
identity: American sociologist who showed that a group changes character at certain proportions. Below a threshold, a lone minority is treated as a symbol, not a person.

name: Sendhil Mullainathan & Eldar Shafir
identity: An economist and a psychologist who showed that scarcity itself — of money, time, or anything — taxes the mind and shrinks the attention left for everything else.

name: Sheena Iyengar
identity: American social psychologist at Columbia Business School whose work mapped when more choice helps and when it hurts.

name: Stafford Beer
identity: British cybernetician who founded the study of how to run organisations as living systems, and gave the field its bluntest diagnostic line.

name: Stephen Denning
identity: Former World Bank executive who turned a single story into a method for leading change through stories.

name: Steven Kerr
identity: American management scholar whose 1975 paper on the gap between what organisations say they value and what they actually reward is among the most-cited in the field.

name: Sun Tzu
identity: The Chinese strategist credited with The Art of War. It is the oldest sustained account of how to act well in a contest you did not choose, with forces you cannot spare, against an opponent you do not control.

name: The Stoics
identity: A philosophy of self-government, begun in Athens around 300 BCE and carried for five centuries — its enduring core preserved by three Romans who each held a very different position of power.

name: Thomas Schelling
identity: American economist who showed that the behaviour of a whole population need look nothing like the behaviour of the people in it.

name: Uri Hasson & Keith Oatley
identity: Two researchers, a neuroscientist and a cognitive psychologist. Their separate work, put side by side, is the strongest evidence we have that a story does something to the mind that an argument does not.

name: Victor Vroom
identity: The organisational psychologist who gave work motivation its sharpest formula. You act when you believe effort pays off, the payoff comes, and the payoff is worth wanting.

name: W. Edwards Deming
identity: American statistician who taught Japan that quality is built into the system, not policed out of the worker.

name: Walter Fisher
identity: American communication theorist; author of the narrative paradigm

name: Warren Buffett
identity: The chairman of Berkshire Hathaway, whose fifty years of shareholder letters are the clearest first-hand record we have of how a large institution actually behaves.

name: Wendy Wood
identity: American social psychologist and the most rigorous living researcher on how habits form — she showed that a habit is built by repeating an action in a stable context, not by willpower.

name: William Bridges
identity: American consultant and writer on the human experience of change — the grief, disorientation and slow re-forming of identity that a change sets off inside people.

name: William Shakespeare
identity: The English playwright whose work maps the human situations of power, ambition and change more fully than any other.
